description: Unspecified vulnerability in ASSA ABLOY APTUS Styra Porttelefonkort 4400 before A2 has unknown impact and attack vectors.
Claude Reasoning: ASSA ABLOY Aptus is an access-control/door-entry system for multi-dwelling residential buildings (this is a door-phone board); it governs building door access but is a professionally-managed building system rather than a consumer home smart lock.

description: ASSA ABLOY Yale WIPC-301W 2.x.2.29 through 2.x.2.43_p1 devices allow Eval Injection of commands.
Claude Reasoning: Yale/ASSA ABLOY WIPC-301W may be a wireless IP peephole camera or door entry panel; the WIPC naming suggests a camera product rather than a door lock, but Yale also sells combined door viewer/lock products; no explicit door lock context in description
Human Notes 2: Yale WIPC-301W is a WiFi door peephole camera — not a door lock; belongs in cameras category.

description: An elevation of privilege vulnerability in Smart Lock could enable a local malicious user to access Smart Lock settings without a PIN. This issue is rated as Moderate because it first requires physical access to an unlocked device where Smart Lock was the last settings pane accessed by the user. Product: Android. Versions: 5.0.2, 5.1.1, 6.0, 6.0.1. Android ID: A-29055171.
Human Notes 2: Android OS Smart Lock phone feature — not a smart door lock device; phone OS software out of scope.

description: Akerun - Smart Lock Robot App for iOS before 1.2.4 does not verify SSL certificates.
Human Notes 2: Phone/watch/wearable sensor — not a standalone home IoT sensor.

description: An authentication bypass in website post requests in the Tzumi Electronics Klic Lock application 1.0.9 for mobile devices allows attackers to access resources (that are not otherwise accessible without proper authentication) via capture-replay. Physically proximate attackers can use this information to unlock unauthorized Tzumi Electronics Klic Smart Padlock Model 5686 Firmware 6.2.
Claude Reasoning: Tzumi Klic Lock is a smart padlock for portable use (bikes, storage), not a residential door lock or garage door opener; the CPE confirms a smart padlock model
Human Notes 2: Portable smart padlock (bike/storage) — not a residential door lock; wrong category.

description: Nokelock Smart padlock O1 Version 5.3.0 is vulnerable to Insecure Permissions. By sending a request, you can add any device and set the device password in the Nokelock app.
Claude Reasoning: Nokelock O1 is a smart padlock for portable use, not a residential door lock; padlocks are used for bikes and storage rather than home entry control
Human Notes 2: Portable smart padlock (bike/storage) — not a residential door lock; wrong category.

description: Tinxy Door Lock with firmware before 3.2 allow attackers to unlock a door by replaying an Unlock request that occurred when the attacker was previously authorized. In other words, door-access revocation is mishandled.
Human Notes 2: Tinxy Smart WiFi Door Lock — consumer home smart lock; in-scope for doorlock category.

description: Missing TLS certificate validation on 3xLogic Infinias eIDC32 devices through 3.4.125 allows an attacker to intercept/control the channel by which door lock policies are applied.
Human Notes 2: 3xLogic Infinias eIDC32 is commercial building access control — not consumer home door lock; out of scope.

description: Z-Wave devices based on Silicon Labs 500 series chipsets using S0 authentication are susceptible to uncontrolled resource consumption leading to battery exhaustion. As an example, the Schlage BE468 version 3.42 door lock is vulnerable and fails open at a low battery level.
Human Notes 2: Silicon Labs Z-Wave/EmberZNet vulnerability with direct consumer smart door lock impact (e.g. Schlage BE468, Door Lock cluster).

description: SGUDA U-Lock central lock control service’s lock management function has incorrect authorization. A remote attacker with general privilege can exploit this vulnerability to call privileged APIs to acquire information, manipulate or disrupt the functionality of arbitrary electronic locks.
Human Notes 2: SGUDA U-Lock is a consumer WiFi smart deadbolt; cloud lock-management API flaw is in-scope for doorlock.

description: Weak encryption mechanisms in RFID Tags in Yale Keyless Lock v1.0 allows attackers to create a cloned tag via physical proximity to the original.
Human Notes 2: Yale Keyless Smart Lock — consumer residential smart lock; in-scope for doorlock category.

description: When the Genie Company Aladdin Connect garage door opener (Retrofit-Kit Model ALDCM) is placed into configuration mode the web servers “Garage Door Control Module Setup” page is vulnerable to XSS via a broadcast SSID name containing malicious code with client side Java Script and/or HTML. This allows the attacker to inject malicious code with client side Java Script and/or HTML into the users' web browser.
Claude Reasoning: The Genie Aladdin Connect Retrofit-Kit is a consumer smart garage-door opener, explicitly within this category's scope ("incl. garage-door openers"); the XSS lives in the device's own web-based setup page, making this a clear in-scope home-access-control vulnerability.
Human Notes 2: Genie Aladdin Connect — consumer smart garage door opener; in-scope for doorlock category.

description: Tinxy WiFi Lock Controller v1 RF was discovered to transmit sensitive information in plaintext, including control information and device credentials, allowing attackers to possibly intercept and access sensitive information via a man-in-the-middle attack.
Human Notes 2: Tinxy Smart WiFi Door Lock — consumer home smart lock; in-scope for doorlock category.

description: Tinxy WiFi Lock Controller v1 RF was discovered to store users' sensitive information, including credentials and mobile phone numbers, in plaintext.
Human Notes 2: Tinxy Smart WiFi Door Lock — consumer home smart lock; in-scope for doorlock category.

description: Tinxy WiFi Lock Controller v1 RF was discovered to be configured to transmit on an open Wi-Fi network, allowing attackers to join the network without authentication.
Human Notes 2: Tinxy Smart WiFi Door Lock — consumer home smart lock; in-scope for doorlock category.

description: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) vulnerability in the OAuth implementation of the Tuya SDK 6.5.0 for Android and iOS, affects the Tuya Smart and Smartlife mobile applications, as well as other third-party applications that integrate the SDK, allows an attacker to link their own Amazon Alexa account to a victim's Tuya account. The applications fail to validate the OAuth state parameter during the account linking flow, enabling a cross-site request forgery (CSRF)-like attack. By tricking the victim into clicking a crafted authorization link, an attacker can complete the OAuth flow on the victim's behalf, resulting in unauthorized Alexa access to the victim's Tuya-connected devices. This affects users regardless of prior Alexa linkage and does not require the Tuya application to be active at the time. Successful exploitation may allow remote control of devices such as cameras, doorbells, door locks, or alarms.
Human Notes 2: Phone/watch/wearable sensor — not a standalone home IoT sensor.

description: In EmberZNet v9.0.2 and earlier, malformed or out-of-range Door Lock user identifiers can trigger out-of-bounds table reads and terminate the process. These messages must come from a device that has already joined the network, and no information leakage back to the sender was observed. Only devices supporting the Door Lock cluster may be impacted.
Claude Reasoning: EmberZNet is Silicon Labs' Zigbee stack used in home IoT devices; the CVE specifically targets the Door Lock cluster which is the Zigbee specification for residential smart door locks; though a protocol stack rather than a specific consumer product, it directly affects home smart lock implementations
Human Notes 2: Silicon Labs Z-Wave/EmberZNet vulnerability with direct consumer smart door lock impact (e.g. Schlage BE468, Door Lock cluster).

description: In EmberZNet v9.0.2 and earlier, malformed ClearWeekdaySchedule messages can trigger out-of-bounds writes into Door Lock schedule state. The size and location of this data is limited. These messages must come from a device that has already joined the network. Only devices supporting the Door Lock cluster may be impacted.
Claude Reasoning: Same as CVE-2026-47149; EmberZNet Zigbee Door Lock cluster schedule vulnerability directly affecting home smart lock implementations via the Zigbee protocol stack
Human Notes 2: Silicon Labs Z-Wave/EmberZNet vulnerability with direct consumer smart door lock impact (e.g. Schlage BE468, Door Lock cluster).

Review Reason: both strong reviewers low confidence; judgments not unanimous
description: Z-Wave devices based on Silicon Labs 500 series chipsets using CRC-16 encapsulation, including but likely not limited to the Linear LB60Z-1 version 3.5, Dome DM501 version 4.26, and Jasco ZW4201 version 4.05, do not implement encryption or replay protection.
Claude Reasoning: Generic Z-Wave 500/700-series chipset/protocol weakness demonstrated on non-lock devices (lamp/appliance modules, sensors, USB controllers, plugs, a hub); no door lock is named, so it does not clearly affect a door lock.
Codex Reasoning: Z-Wave chipset/protocol flaw affecting consumer smart-lock ecosystems.
Gemini Reasoning: The devices listed (Linear LB60Z-1, and others) as Z-Wave devices, but they are not smart locks.

description: Z-Wave devices based on Silicon Labs 500 series chipsets using S2, including but likely not limited to the ZooZ ZST10 version 6.04, ZooZ ZEN20 version 5.03, ZooZ ZEN25 version 5.03, Aeon Labs ZW090-A version 3.95, and Fibaro FGWPB-111 version 4.3, are susceptible to denial of service and resource exhaustion via malformed SECURITY NONCE GET, SECURITY NONCE GET 2, NO OPERATION, or NIF REQUEST messages.
Claude Reasoning: Generic Z-Wave 500/700-series chipset/protocol weakness demonstrated on non-lock devices (lamp/appliance modules, sensors, USB controllers, plugs, a hub); no door lock is named, so it does not clearly affect a door lock.
Gemini Reasoning: The devices listed (ZooZ ZST10, ZEN20, ZEN25, Aeon Labs ZW090-A, Fibaro FGWPB-111) are Z-Wave sticks, encrypted switches, and power monitors, not smart locks.

description: Z-Wave devices using Silicon Labs 500 and 700 series chipsets, including but not likely limited to the SiLabs UZB-7 version 7.00, ZooZ ZST10 version 6.04, Aeon Labs ZW090-A version 3.95, and Samsung STH-ETH-200 version 6.04, are susceptible to denial of service via malformed routing messages.
Claude Reasoning: Generic Z-Wave 500/700-series chipset/protocol weakness demonstrated on non-lock devices (lamp/appliance modules, sensors, USB controllers, plugs, a hub); no door lock is named, so it does not clearly affect a door lock.
Gemini Reasoning: The devices listed (SiLabs UZB-7, ZooZ ZST10, Zoo uma ZST10, Samsung STH-ETH-200// a hub/gateway), are not smart locks.

description: SGUDA U-Lock central lock control service’s user management function has incorrect authorization. A remote attacker with general user privilege can exploit this vulnerability to call privileged APIs to access, modify and delete user information.
